food_name: Braised Scaly Hedgehog Mushrooms with Pork Belly
aliases: Braised Hedgehog Mushrooms with Pork Belly

english: Braised Hedgehog Mushrooms with Pork Belly

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for Sarcodon imbricatus braised dish specifically. Mapped to the generic braised food product class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Sarcodon imbricatus braised with pork belly. A00GJ (cooked fungi) is used as the closest defensible anchor. The pork belly component is not captured in this single code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific traditional Chinese braised mushroom-pork preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of braised scaly hedgehog mushrooms with pork belly is approximately 250 g, typically plated as a main dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork belly, raw (FDC ID 168289) and Mushrooms, cooked (FDC ID 169251); values combined and adjusted for braising with soy sauce and sugar
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct entry for braised Sarcodon imbricatus with pork belly. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked pork belly (FDC ID 168289) and cooked mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) in approximate proportions consistent with the dish description (roughly 55% pork belly, 35% mushrooms, 10% braising liquid contribution), then adjusted for sodium from dark soy sauce and sugar from rock sugar. The input AI-generated values (protein 8.5 g, fat 16.5 g, carbohydrate 6.8 g, energy 210 kcal per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Saturated fat estimated from USDA pork belly fat profile (~6.0 g per 100 g of dish). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not reliably derivable for this composite preparation and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of mixed pork and mushroom braised dish — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised Sarcodon imbricatus with pork belly. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the macronutrient profile: the dish is predominantly protein and fat (from pork belly), with low net carbohydrate (6.8 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (1.8 g), and fat content that substantially slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. This is consistent with GI values reported for high-fat, high-protein, low-carbohydrate mixed meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 6.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.95 g (rounded), classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg per 100 g; ~1125 mg per 250 g serving) from dark soy sauce braising liquid, combined with high saturated fat from pork belly
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (~1125 mg per serving, approaching 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit) and high saturated fat from pork belly; if consumed, portion size should be reduced and paired with low-sodium accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and low net carbohydrate (6.8 g per 100 g), but high total fat and saturated fat content from pork belly may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI and low carbohydrate content are favorable for glycemic control in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat content (~6 g per 100 g) warrants portion moderation, and the rock sugar in the braising liquid should be accounted for in total carbohydrate tracking.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from pork belly (~6 g per 100 g) and high sodium (450 mg per 100 g) are both established cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions, given its high saturated fat content which raises LDL cholesterol and its high sodium content which elevates blood pressure; substituting leaner cuts of pork or reducing the pork-to-mushroom ratio would improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and sodium from pork belly and soy sauce may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of this dish as the high saturated fat from pork belly and elevated sodium from soy sauce may exacerbate inflammatory pathways; the wild mushroom component provides beneficial beta-glucans and antioxidants but does not offset the pro-inflammatory lipid profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Scaly Hedgehog Mushrooms with Pork Belly (红烧齿菌, Hóng Shāo Chǐ Jūn) is a traditional rustic main dish from the forested regions of Southwest China, particularly Yunnan Province. The dish features Sarcodon imbricatus — the scaly hedgehog mushroom, known locally as Hei Niu Gan Jun — a rare, meaty wild mushroom with a firm, sponge-like texture, a slightly bitter and nutty flavour, and a robust umami depth. The mushrooms are braised alongside thick slabs of pork belly in a savory-sweet braising liquid made from dark soy sauce, rock sugar, and star anise. During the long braising process, the mushrooms absorb the rendered pork fat and the aromatic braising liquid, which mellows their natural bitterness and infuses the pork with an earthy, forest-like complexity. The result is a rich, deeply flavoured main course where the mushrooms serve as a co-centrepiece alongside the tender, lacquered pork belly. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 210 kcal per 100 g, with 8.5 g protein, 16.5 g fat (predominantly from pork belly), and 6.8 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 450 mg per 100 g due to the soy sauce braising liquid, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), reflecting the dominant protein-fat macronutrient profile and the fibre contribution from the mushrooms. This dish is best suited as an occasional indulgence for those on heart-healthy or low-sodium diets, and is traditionally served at lunch or dinner as a centrepiece main course, particularly during foraging season when fresh Sarcodon imbricatus mushrooms are available.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for braised Sarcodon imbricatus with pork belly. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked pork belly and cooked mushrooms in proportions consistent with the dish description. The AI-generated input values are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this composite braised preparation (Sarcodon imbricatus with pork belly in soy-sugar braising liquid) are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (6.0 g) is estimated from USDA pork belly fat profile. 2. The input source cited ('Yunnan Academy of Agricultural Sciences') is not on the approved source list per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved evidence anchor. 3. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical '1 serving (250g)' entries) has been deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology for high-fat, high-protein, low-carbohydrate mixed meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the approved GI database. 5. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 17.0) / 100 = 5.95 g (low GL), where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 6.8 × 2.5 = 17.0 g. 6. This dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 7. Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this specific dish.
